summary: Microsoft Support publishes ThreatFileHashLogging = 1 under HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows Defender as the Windows Defender Antivirus root policy for file-hash logging, and the Defender-on 25H2 VM shows MsMpEng.exe reading that root value directly. The current build still has extra live aliases and the PE follow-up still did not surface event 1120, but the app can now stay on the documented root path instead of shipping a research batch.

setting.name: Microsoft Defender Threat File Hash Logging
setting.casual_explanation: Defender can compute and log file hashes for some file-based detections, but the current 25H2 engine still exposes more than one registry surface for the setting.
setting.why_people_change_it: People change this when they are researching Defender file indicators, event 1120, or file-hash logging behavior.
setting.tradeoffs_overview: The documented root policy is live on 25H2 and is now enough for an app-ready toggle. The current build still has extra alias surfaces and the PE follow-up still left the event 1120 behavior unresolved.
setting.professional_notes: Microsoft Support publishes ThreatFileHashLogging = 1 under HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows Defender as the Windows Defender Antivirus registry setting for SHA1 threat-file hash logging, and the same article ties the setting to event 1120. Microsoft Defender for Endpoint also documents file-hash computation on the MpEngine policy path and says the feature applies to PE files only. In the Defender-on Win25H2Clean snapshot, a baseline EICAR text-file probe produced event 1116 and no event 1120. After writing ThreatFileHashLogging = 1 on the root Defender policy key, MsMpEng.exe queried that exact value with Data: 1 and also checked the Policy Manager and non-policy MpEngine aliases as NAME NOT FOUND. In a second run, writing Policy Manager\EnableFileHashComputation = 1 made MsMpEng.exe query that alias directly with Data: 1 while the legacy root value stayed NAME NOT FOUND. A non-rebooted pass on the documented Policies\...\MpEngine path still produced no direct read. A follow-up service restart attempt was blocked because WinDefend could not be stopped from the guest, and a rebooted capture pass still showed MsMpEng.exe reading only the non-policy HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows Defender\MpEngine branch for ring values, not EnableFileHashComputation on the documented policy path. A later follow-up switched from the text-file probe and the failed PE-overlay test to Microsoft's password-protected BaFS Sample PE file. That official PE sample triggered event 1116 on the baseline and on live root and Policy Manager passes, but still produced no event 1120. In those PE passes the filtered Procmon window did not show a fresh direct read of ThreatFileHashLogging or Policy Manager\EnableFileHashComputation, and the rebooted MpEngine pass still showed MATCH_COUNT=0 with no event 1120. The app now stays on the documented root ThreatFileHashLogging path for the supported surface, while the Policy Manager alias and quiet MpEngine path remain current-build research notes rather than part of the app contract.

allowed_values.label: Not configured
allowed_values.meaning: Leave the legacy root surface unset.

allowed_values.label: Legacy root disabled
allowed_values.meaning: Turns off the legacy root flag on the older ThreatFileHashLogging surface.

allowed_values.label: Legacy root enabled
allowed_values.meaning: Makes MsMpEng.exe read ThreatFileHashLogging = 1 directly from the root Defender policy key in the 25H2 VM.

targets.notes: This is the legacy dump-visible surface. It is live on the current 25H2 VM, but Microsoft's current docs do not present it as the canonical modern path.

allowed_values.label: Not configured
allowed_values.meaning: Leave the Policy Manager alias unset.

allowed_values.label: Policy Manager alias disabled
allowed_values.meaning: Turns off the Policy Manager alias for file-hash computation.

allowed_values.label: Policy Manager alias enabled
allowed_values.meaning: Makes MsMpEng.exe read EnableFileHashComputation = 1 from the Policy Manager alias in the 25H2 VM.

targets.notes: This alias is visible in upstream dumps and is live on the current 25H2 VM.

allowed_values.label: Not configured
allowed_values.meaning: Leave the documented modern MpEngine surface unset.

allowed_values.label: Documented MpEngine path disabled
allowed_values.meaning: Turns off the documented modern MpEngine path.

allowed_values.label: Documented MpEngine path enabled
allowed_values.meaning: Microsoft documents this as the supported file-hash-computation policy state, but the 25H2 probe did not produce a direct read from this path in either the non-rebooted or rebooted capture pass.

targets.notes: Microsoft documents this path, but the current 25H2 VM did not show a direct read from it in the non-rebooted pass, the service-restart attempt, or the rebooted capture follow-up.

app_current_implementation.notes: The app now writes only the documented Defender root ThreatFileHashLogging value. The Policy Manager alias remains in research as a live 25H2 observation, not as part of the app contract.

windows_defaults.label: Policy unset baseline
windows_defaults.applies_to: Systems where all known file-hash-computation policy surfaces stay unset

states.rationale: Leave the legacy root surface unset.
Leave the Policy Manager alias unset.
Leave the documented MpEngine path unset.

recommended_profiles.label: Windows default
intended_for: Systems that are not actively researching Defender file indicators

states.rationale: Keep the legacy root surface unset.
Keep the Policy Manager alias unset.
Keep the documented MpEngine path unset until the active control surface is tighter.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want the least-assumptive Defender baseline.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You need a controlled file-hash logging experiment.
tradeoffs: Leaves all known file-hash policy surfaces alone.
Does not force the documented root policy for Defender file-hash logging.

recommended_profiles.label: Current app policy
intended_for: Systems that need the documented Windows Defender root policy

states.rationale: Use the documented Windows Defender Antivirus root policy that the 25H2 VM also read directly.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want the documented root Defender policy for file-hash logging.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You need to compare every current-build alias surface in the same run.
tradeoffs: The root policy path is official and produced a direct live read on 25H2.
The current build still has extra alias surfaces and the PE follow-up still left event 1120 unresolved.

evidence.title: Microsoft Support: March 2016 anti-malware platform update for Endpoint Protection clients
evidence.summary: Microsoft Support publishes ThreatFileHashLogging = 1 under HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows Defender as the Windows Defender Antivirus registry setting for SHA1 threat-file hash logging and says event 1120 is recorded when a threat file is detected and hash logging is enabled.

evidence.title: Microsoft Learn: Create indicators for files
evidence.summary: Microsoft says file-hash computation is enabled by the Defender Antivirus MpEngine policy 'Enable File Hash Computation' and notes that file indicators support PE files only.

evidence.title: Microsoft support: Troubleshoot Microsoft Defender Antivirus settings
evidence.summary: Microsoft documents event 1120 as the file-hash-logging event and says it is logged only when ThreatFileHashLogging is enabled.

evidence.title: Microsoft Learn: Demonstrate cloud-delivered protection
evidence.summary: Microsoft publishes a password-protected BaFS Sample PE file for Defender cloud-protection demonstrations. The sample used in the VM pass extracted to a 123904-byte PE with SHA256 670b00e90a7c9eb7ac6674441551e7764a8364c26e44dcc92474a9abcfac4c04.

evidence.title: Local Defender dumps and traces for ThreatFileHashLogging and EnableFileHashComputation
evidence.summary: The local dump mirrors show ThreatFileHashLogging on the Defender root path, EnableFileHashComputation on the Policy Manager alias, and the 25H2 trace mirror also shows the non-policy MpEngine surface.

evidence.title: Original high-risk snapshot had Defender disabled
evidence.summary: The original high-risk snapshot had WinDefend and WdNisSvc set to Disabled and Get-MpComputerStatus reported 'Not running', which would have made Defender policy validation misleading until the VM was repaired.

evidence.title: Defender-on 25H2 snapshot baseline
evidence.summary: After re-enabling WinDefend and waiting for the service to settle, Get-MpComputerStatus reported Normal with real-time protection, behavior monitoring, and IOAV protection enabled. A dedicated Defender-on snapshot was then created for this lane.

evidence.title: Defender-on baseline EICAR probe
evidence.summary: With all known file-hash-computation policy surfaces unset, the Defender-on 25H2 VM produced event 1116 for the EICAR text-file probe and no event 1120.

evidence.title: MsMpEng.exe direct read of ThreatFileHashLogging = 1
evidence.summary: With ThreatFileHashLogging = 1 on the root Defender policy path, MsMpEng.exe queried HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows Defender\ThreatFileHashLogging and read Data: 1 directly.

evidence.title: MsMpEng.exe direct read of Policy Manager EnableFileHashComputation = 1
evidence.summary: With EnableFileHashComputation = 1 on the Policy Manager alias, MsMpEng.exe queried HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows Defender\Policy Manager\EnableFileHashComputation and read Data: 1 directly while the legacy root value stayed NAME NOT FOUND.

evidence.title: Non-rebooted MpEngine pass did not show a live read
evidence.summary: Setting HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows Defender\MpEngine\EnableFileHashComputation = 1 did not produce a live registry read or event 1120 in the same non-rebooted EICAR text-file pass.

evidence.title: WinDefend service restart follow-up was blocked
evidence.summary: A follow-up pass requested a WinDefend restart before the EICAR probe, but Defender service control was blocked in the guest and the capture still produced event 1116 with no event 1120.

evidence.title: Rebooted MpEngine pass still did not show a direct policy-path read
evidence.summary: After pre-writing HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows Defender\MpEngine\EnableFileHashComputation = 1 and rebooting the guest, the capture still showed MATCH_COUNT=0 for EnableFileHashComputation or ThreatFileHashLogging while MsMpEng.exe continued reading the non-policy HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows Defender\MpEngine branch for ring values.

evidence.title: Official Microsoft PE sample baseline still produced no event 1120
evidence.summary: Using Microsoft's BaFS Sample PE file on the Defender-on 25H2 baseline produced event 1116 for Trojan:Win32/Clengtst.A!plock and still produced no event 1120.

evidence.title: Legacy root PE pass still produced no event 1120
evidence.summary: With ThreatFileHashLogging = 1 and the official Microsoft PE sample, the 25H2 VM still produced event 1116 and no event 1120. The filtered Procmon summary did not show a fresh direct read of ThreatFileHashLogging in the same PE detection window.

evidence.title: Policy Manager PE pass still produced no event 1120
evidence.summary: With Policy Manager\EnableFileHashComputation = 1 and the official Microsoft PE sample, the 25H2 VM still produced event 1116 and no event 1120. The filtered Procmon summary did not show a fresh direct read of EnableFileHashComputation in the same PE detection window.

evidence.title: Rebooted MpEngine PE pass still produced no event 1120
evidence.summary: After pre-writing the documented MpEngine value, rebooting, and using the official Microsoft PE sample, the 25H2 VM still showed MATCH_COUNT=0, event 1120 stayed absent, and the copied sample was already gone by the time the activity collector checked FILE_EXISTS_AFTER.

decision.why: Microsoft publishes the exact root ThreatFileHashLogging registry path for Windows Defender Antivirus, the 25H2 VM shows MsMpEng.exe reading that root value directly, and the app now writes only that documented root path. The extra Policy Manager alias and the quiet MpEngine path still matter as current-build research, but they no longer block the app-ready root policy surface.

open_questions: Is the legacy root ThreatFileHashLogging value still the effective runtime switch on current consumer 25H2 builds, or is it only one of several aliases?
Is the Policy Manager alias the real active 25H2 policy surface while the documented policy MpEngine path is stale or enterprise-specific on this build?
What extra condition causes event 1120 on this build if the official Microsoft BaFS Sample PE file is not enough by itself?

validation_proof.exact_quote_or_path: MsMpEng.exe | RegQueryValue | HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows Defender\ThreatFileHashLogging | SUCCESS | Type: REG_DWORD, Length: 4, Data: 1
validation_proof.notes: The Defender-on 25H2 VM produced a clean baseline event 1116 with no event 1120. Earlier text-file passes showed MsMpEng.exe reading the root ThreatFileHashLogging value directly and reading the Policy Manager EnableFileHashComputation alias directly in a separate pass. The app now stays on the documented root policy path. The later official Microsoft PE sample follow-up still produced event 1116 with no event 1120, so the hash-event behavior remains an open current-build question rather than part of the app contract.
